replies.author: @robertoj.9509
replies.text:
@jfbsp1893 Then he doesn't understand Superman at all. You just don't use Superman to show "Oh, look how good he is!" Superman comics have never been about bragging how good, how heroic, or how perfect he is. Superman comics are about his struggle to fit Earth, the burden to want to fix things and knowing that a major intervention could either help or ruin human's future. Not knowing his limits, and feeling like an alien. Lois, as matter of fact, is the only thing he considers that makes him feel "Human". She's the anchor. Take her away, and Superman loses all his humanity (this is already explained in comics and future versions of him, and also explained by the Legion in many comics), because despite him being a symbol of hope, his hope is not so strong... Blue Lanterns told him that. He needed "training" to become a great Blue Lantern, but a training he doesn't need because he already symbolizes hope despite his flaws and weaknesses. 

So, in an essence, trying to make Superman look "Human"; is stupid. He already realized he's not human. He will never be. And what he needs to learn is to adapt and get to live with people that are so different than him, both biologically and morally.

author: @MonkeySlayer94
text:
6:09 For me, that right there is a major concern because that type of creative decision was the same mistake the SnyderVerse (good riddance) made. The previous DC film continuity was built on a weak foundation because it was too focused on building a cinematic universe when the appropriate approach is giving their characters their own solo films first, with an interconnected linchpin here and there, before heading into crossover territory. The only other recent film franchise to do this right is The MonsterVerse, giving Godzilla and Kong their own films before crossing them over in the fourth installment, and it's the second most successful cinematic universe as a result.

This isn't a rule the MCU introduced, that honor belongs to the classic Toho kaijū films of the Shōwa era, but it's the one they popularized and firmly established for every C.U. in general, so I don't get DC's hesitation to follow this simple rule.

I'm still interested in this new movie. We'll see.
